does: This paper introduces Diffusion-DPO, a method for aligning text-to-image diffusion models with human preferences by directly optimizing on pairwise comparison data. The authors adapt Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) from the LLM domain to the diffusion model setting, reformulating it to work with diffusion model likelihoods using the evidence lower bound to create a differentiable objective. They fine-tune Stable Diffusion using the Pick-a-Pic dataset containing 851K crowdsourced pairwise image preferences, demonstrating that this direct preference optimization approach improves both visual appeal and text-image alignment without requiring explicit reward models or reinforcement learning.
connects: This work extends the recent success of Direct Preference Optimization in language models to the generative image domain, building on the shift away from complex RLHF methods toward simpler direct preference learning. It responds to the gap where human preference learning has been underexplored in diffusion models compared to LLMs, with prior work limited to supervised fine-tuning on curated datasets. The paper connects to broader research on aligning generative models with human values and preferences, while adapting the theoretical framework of DPO—which itself built on insights from preference learning and implicit reward modeling—to the unique mathematical structure of diffusion models and their probabilistic objectives.
enables: This work opens the door to preference-based alignment methods for generative image models, potentially scaling to larger datasets and more complex preference signals beyond current visual quality metrics. It suggests future directions for combining human feedback with diffusion models across other modalities and tasks, and demonstrates that DPO's elegant formulation can generalize beyond the language domain. The approach enables more efficient alignment without training separate reward models, potentially leading to practical systems where diffusion models continuously adapt to user preferences. It also points toward investigating how different types of preference data—aesthetic, semantic, safety-related—can be integrated to create more nuanced and controllable image generation systems.
